transcription.text: Cristiano Ronaldo posts a photo, 5 million people like it in 10 minutes.
Imagine 5 million people trying to walk through one door at the same time.
That door breaks.
So Instagram doesn't use one door, they use hundreds.
A traffic cop stands at the front, splitting the crowd evenly across every door so none of them break.
That traffic cop is a load balancer.
But even with hundreds of doors, you still can't write 5 million likes to a database one by one.
The system would choke, so Instagram doesn't even try.
The moment you tap the heart, your phone turns it red immediately.
But officially, your like is simply written down line by line in a massive, fast-moving logbook.
It's just an endless list of actions.
No rushing, no crashing.
That logbook is an event stream.
Now, you want to see the total count update instantly.
So, a fast worker constantly reads that logbook and updates a whiteboard near the front door.
The whiteboard is incredibly fast and always up to date.
That whiteboard is the cash.
Famous posts live on it permanently.
But what about the official record?
We don't throw the logbook away when the whiteboard is updated.
A second worker, a quiet, steady clerk, is reading that exact same logbook.
Every few seconds, the clerk gathers up thousands of new likes from the log,
bundles them together, and officially saves them into the permanent vault, the database.
Four pieces working together.
The load balancer splits the crowd.
The event stream catches the tsunami of likes.
The cash shows you the fast count.
The database secures the permanent record.
The official name is eventual consistency.
The real record catches up a little later, not instantly.
But you never notice.
And that's the whole point.
